select_when: Actions and consequences are separated in time, so a naive short-term read could mistake no result yet for no effect.
A system contains feedback loops, capacity constraints, or implementation lags that could trigger overreaction if ignored.
The team needs to distinguish between a pause that improves judgment and a delay that merely avoids commitment or ownership.
Performance is being judged too quickly even though the real signal depends on delayed feedback.
avoid_when: People are romanticizing waiting and reflection even though the next useful move is already clear and the real cost is execution drift.
The model is being used to excuse planning drag instead of to understand timing, lagged feedback, or paced learning.

curation_notes.summary: Kept the model centered on lagged consequences, pacing, and overreaction risk rather than on broad time-management advice.
donor_drops: Did not preserve donor time-management sprawl because the raw source is strongest on delayed feedback, pacing, and implementation lag.
Did not add higher-order composition fields because this batch stays inside the frozen Wave 1 to Wave 3 contracts.
open_questions: The second avoid-when item is reviewed normalization over a source that is stronger on anti-romanticizing delay than on explicit anti-activation routing.

select_when.note: The raw source is explicit that delays matter most when consequences arrive later than the action and feedback loops create overreaction risk.

avoid_when.note: The anti-romanticizing warning is explicit; the execution-drift phrasing is conservative reviewed normalization.

input_type.note: The raw model activates on lag and timing problems, but the compact input label is reviewed operational normalization.

output_type.note: The source yields a better frame for pacing and lagged consequences; the output label compresses that into a typed artifact.

reasoning_types.note: Delays clearly work through causal timing and systems feedback, but the taxonomy labels remain reviewed normalization.
